title: Routing the overflow from a water butt
subtitle: Connecting a second butt or diverting overflow away from foundations
excerpt: A water butt fills in a single heavy shower and then overflows onto the same patch of ground every time it rains, which can cause waterlogging and damage nearby footings. Two fixes: connect a second butt in series, or route the overflow to a soakaway or planted bed.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on water butt installation and rainwater harvesting. Recoup water energy recycling technical notes on domestic rainwater management.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pit filled with rubble or aggregate into which surface water is directed. The water percolates slowly into the surrounding ground rather than running off into drains or pooling on the surface.

projectSchedule.title: Check connections for leaks after first rain
projectSchedule.body: After the first rainfall since installation, check the overflow connection point and the tap fittings on both butts. Tighten any gland nut that is weeping. A slow drip from a gland nut can be cured with a half-turn of the nut; if it persists, add PTFE tape to the thread.

content.text: A standard 200-litre water butt fills completely from the roof area of a typical single-storey extension during a 10 mm rainfall event. Once full, the overflow outlet discharges at the base of the downpipe. If not routed away, it saturates the ground beside the house wall. This can damage cavity insulation, brick pointing, and in some cases footings.
Option 1: connecting a second butt in series

A second butt connected at the overflow of the first doubles the catchment capacity before overflow reaches the ground. The connection is a short length of 25 mm overflow pipe from the overflow outlet of butt one to the inlet bung of butt two. Both butts need to be at the same height, or butt two slightly lower, so that gravity fills them in sequence.
Position the second butt adjacent to the first, on a level base (paving slabs or compacted gravel). Both butts must be stable when full.
Drill a hole in the upper body of butt two at the same height as the overflow outlet on butt one. Fit a grommet and overflow connector into the hole.
Connect the two overflow outlets with the overflow pipe. Ensure the pipe runs level or slightly downhill from butt one to butt two.
Fit a standard tap to butt two if it does not already have one.
Option 2: routing overflow to a soakaway or planted bed
If space for a second butt is not available, route the overflow away from the building using a short length of overflow pipe directed to a
or a planted bed at least 1.5 m from the wall. Most water butt diverters use 25 mm overflow pipe. A short run of this pipe angled downhill is enough.

A simple soakaway for this purpose is a 30 cm diameter hole 50 cm deep, backfilled with 20 mm gravel. In most garden soils this drains faster than any realistic overflow rate from a water butt. In heavy clay soils, direct the overflow to the lowest planted bed instead; the water will be absorbed by the root zone rather than puddling on the surface.
The diverter and the butt level

Most downpipe water butt diverters have a self-closing mechanism that routes water back to the downpipe once the butt is full. This prevents overflow at the butt but does not change where the water goes: it simply returns to the drain. For households where the drain connection is near the house wall, routing the overflow away from the house reduces the volume of water saturating that one spot during heavy rain.
